heroHeadline: Suu Kyi Remains Imprisoned as EAOs Reject SAC Peace Talks; Cross-Border Hospital Bomb Triggers Diplomatic Fallout; Homalin Port Fire Kills 2
heroSubtitle: On April 22, international pressure on Myanmar's junta intensified as the diplomatic fallout from the April 20 cross-border bombing of a KNU hospital in Bue So (Karen State) continued — one bomb landed in Thailand's Mae Hong Son Province and six Arakan Army members were killed. Thailand demanded explanation while UN bodies condemned the strike on a protected medical facility. Major ethnic armed organizations, including the KNU and CNF, publicly rejected Min Aung Hlaing's July peace talks invitation, calling it illegitimate absent an end to military operations. Internationally, scrutiny mounted over the junta's selective Thingyan amnesty: former President U Win Myint was freed April 17 while Aung San Suu Kyi — sentence reduced from 27 to 22.5 years — remained imprisoned on Day 1,906. On April 21, a fire at Homalin's river port (Sagaing) killed 2 and injured 11, destroying 22 boats — the second major port disaster in Homalin in 2026. The TNLA's April 15 statement welcoming Min Aung Hlaing's presidency (attributed to Chinese pressure) remains the key fault line fracturing the Three Brotherhood Alliance, while the SCEF coalition of NUG, KIO, KNU, CNF, and KNPP continues resistance operations.
footerNote: Data sourced from AAPP, ACLED, UN OCHA, Irrawaddy, RFA, Myanmar Now, DVB, Al Jazeera, Mizzima, Human Rights Watch, Crisis Group, Myanmar Peace Monitor, MoeMaKa, Khaosod English, The Nation Thailand, Foreign Policy, Xinhua, BERNAMA, Anadolu Agency, IMNA/Mon News, FDD, and official NUG, KNU, KIA, and AA statements.
